name: Nausea & Vomiting

key_questions: Onset and duration? Associated with food/timing?
Possible causes: pregnancy (first trimester), viral gastroenteritis, medications, motion sickness, GERD, post-op?
Pregnancy test in women of reproductive age — mandatory
Signs of dehydration: dry mouth, decreased urine output, dizziness?
Recent food exposures (food poisoning), sick contacts (gastroenteritis)?
red_flags: Severe abdominal pain → appendicitis, bowel obstruction, pancreatitis — ER
Hematemesis (vomiting blood) or coffee-ground emesis → upper GI bleed — ER
Projectile vomiting (no nausea before) → central cause (raised ICP)
Signs of severe dehydration or electrolyte abnormality
DKA: fruity breath, polyuria, polydipsia, history of T1DM → ER
Increased intracranial pressure: headache (especially morning/positional), papilledema, vomiting without nausea → ER
ddx: Cannabis hyperemesis syndrome (cyclical vomiting relieved by hot showers — ask about cannabis use frequency and pattern)
Cyclic vomiting syndrome (recurrent stereotyped episodes, often with migraine history)

first_line.name: Scope Note
first_line.notes: ⚠️ General nausea/vomiting and nausea/vomiting of pregnancy (NVP) are NOT in the Ontario MA prescribing scope. Pharmacists provide OTC counselling, referral, and adapt existing prescriptions.

first_line.name: Dimenhydrinate (Gravol)
first_line.drugs: Dimenhydrinate (Gravol) 25–50 mg q4–6h PRN | Gravol Ginger (natural health product)
first_line.notes: H1 antihistamine + anticholinergic — effective for motion sickness and general nausea. OTC. Sedating (use with caution in elderly — falls; Beers Criteria). Avoid in BPH, glaucoma. Pregnancy: dimenhydrinate 50 mg q4–6h has a long safety record and is included in the SOGC 2016 NVP algorithm after pyridoxine ± doxylamine. Acceptable if preferred by patient. Gravol Ginger: natural product, safe in pregnancy.
first_line.preferred_when: Motion sickness, mild-moderate nausea from various causes, short-term use

first_line.name: Pyridoxine (Vitamin B6) — NVP First Step
first_line.drugs: Pyridoxine (vitamin B6) 10–25 mg q6–8h (up to 200 mg/day)
first_line.notes: Per SOGC Guideline No. 344: pyridoxine alone is the FIRST pharmacological step for nausea and vomiting of pregnancy before adding doxylamine. OTC; safe in pregnancy. If insufficient, add doxylamine (Diclectin).
first_line.preferred_when: First-line pharmacotherapy for NVP before escalating to doxylamine-pyridoxine combination

first_line.name: Diclectin (Pregnancy-specific — PRESCRIPTION ONLY)
first_line.drugs: Doxylamine 10 mg + pyridoxine 10 mg (Diclectin) — delayed-release tablets
first_line.notes: ⚠️ Diclectin is PRESCRIPTION-ONLY in Canada — it is not OTC. Pharmacists can recommend it be prescribed but CANNOT prescribe it under Ontario MA. Health Canada approved for nausea/vomiting of pregnancy. Standard titration: Day 1 — 2 tabs at bedtime; Day 2 — add 1 tab in AM; Day 3 — add 1 tab mid-afternoon (max 4 tabs/day = 2 qhs + 1 AM + 1 mid-afternoon). Take 30 min before bed on empty stomach (delayed-release formulation). Safe — extensive safety data in pregnancy.
first_line.preferred_when: Nausea and vomiting of pregnancy (NVP) — after pyridoxine alone insufficient; requires physician/NP prescription

second_line.name: Prochlorperazine
second_line.drugs: Prochlorperazine (Stemetil) 5–10 mg TID–QID PO or PR
second_line.notes: Dopamine antagonist (phenothiazine) — effective antiemetic widely used in Canada. EPS/dystonia risk — avoid in young patients if possible; use lowest effective dose. Available in oral and suppository formulations.

second_line.name: Promethazine
second_line.drugs: Promethazine 12.5–25 mg q4–6h PO or IM
second_line.notes: Phenothiazine antihistamine — effective for nausea/vomiting and motion sickness. Sedating. EPS and respiratory depression risk at higher doses. Available oral and injectable.

second_line.name: Metoclopramide
second_line.notes: Dopamine antagonist + prokinetic. Effective for gastroparesis, chemotherapy nausea, post-op nausea. ⚠️ BLACK BOX WARNING: risk of tardive dyskinesia — limit use to ≤12 weeks; do not exceed recommended dose. EPS risk — use short-term only. Avoid in patients with Parkinson's disease.

second_line.name: Ondansetron
second_line.notes: 5-HT3 antagonist — highly effective for chemotherapy and post-op nausea. Increasing use for NVP (modest safety data, generally considered low risk after first trimester). QTc prolongation concern at high doses. ⚠️ Serotonin syndrome caution: use with care when combined with other serotonergic drugs (SSRIs, SNRIs, tramadol, triptans) — monitor for agitation, tachycardia, hyperthermia, clonus.

non_pharm: Oral rehydration: clear fluids in small sips (Pedialyte, diluted sports drink, clear broth)

Early refeeding: early resumption of normal age-appropriate diet is preferred — BRAT diet (Bananas, Rice, Applesauce, Toast) is outdated and provides inadequate nutrition (per AAP/CPS); bland foods acceptable initially but return to regular diet as tolerated
Ginger (tea, ginger ale, ginger candies): evidence-based for nausea in pregnancy and chemotherapy
Small frequent meals; avoid spicy/fatty foods
P6 (Neiguan) acupressure wristbands: modest evidence for motion sickness and pregnancy nausea
Fresh air, avoid strong smells, stay hydrated
refer_when: Severe dehydration — unable to keep any fluids down (IV fluids needed)
Hematemesis or coffee-ground emesis — upper GI bleed emergency
Severe abdominal pain — surgical emergency (appendicitis, obstruction, pancreatitis)
Hyperemesis gravidarum — weight loss >5%, ketonuria, dehydration — requires IV fluids and hospital assessment
Suspected cannabis hyperemesis syndrome — refer for cessation counselling and medical follow-up
Signs of raised intracranial pressure or DKA — emergency

references: Ontario College of Pharmacists. Minor Ailments: Nausea and Vomiting. OCP Prescribing Standards, O. Reg. 256/24 (2025).
RxTx (Canadian Pharmacists Association). Nausea and Vomiting — Therapeutic Choices, 8th ed. (2023).
ACG (American College of Gastroenterology). ACG Clinical Guideline: Nausea and Vomiting of Pregnancy. Am J Gastroenterol. 2022;117(10):1538–1558.
NICE. Nausea/vomiting in pregnancy: Clinical Knowledge Summary (CKS). National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (2023).
